description:
You are a network engineer who needs to keep an eye on local-host reachability.  
    1.  Create the directory “/home/user/network_logs”.  
    2.  Add a user-level cron entry that runs every two minutes.  
        • The cron entry must be the *only* new line you add and must be exactly (including spacing):  
          */2 * * * * ping -c 1 127.0.0.1 >> /home/user/network_logs/ping.log 2>&1  
    3.  Immediately execute the very same ping command once, so that “/home/user/network_logs/ping.log” contains at least one successful ping result *before* the cron daemon has a chance to fire.  
    4.  Create a short, human-readable summary file called “/home/user/network_logs/last_run.txt” whose contents follow **this mandatory five-line format** (line-breaks matter):  

          Line-1: Last 4 lines of ping.log:  
          Line-2: <verbatim copy of the 1st of the last 4 lines from ping.log>  
          Line-3: <verbatim copy of the 2nd of the last 4 lines from ping.log>  
          Line-4: <verbatim copy of the 3rd of the last 4 lines from ping.log>  
          Line-5: <verbatim copy of the 4th of the last 4 lines from ping.log>  

       • “verbatim” means you must copy each of the last four lines from “ping.log” *exactly* as they appear (including any leading spaces).  
       • Make sure there are no extra blank lines before, after, or in-between the five lines.  

    Success criteria the automatic checker will use:  
    • Directory “/home/user/network_logs” exists.  
    • File “/home/user/network_logs/ping.log” exists and is non-empty.  
    • File “/home/user/network_logs/last_run.txt” exists and has exactly five lines; the very first line is “Last 4 lines of ping.log:” (case-sensitive).  
    • Your personal crontab (obtained with `crontab -l`) contains the single, exact cron line shown above.  

    Hints (don’t print these):  
    • Use `crontab -e` or `crontab -l | { … } | crontab -` to edit the cron table.  
    • To extract the last four lines of a file, think about `tail -n 4`.  
    • Verify file line counts with `wc -l`.
truth:
The test harness will verify:

    1. The path “/home/user/network_logs” is a directory.
    2. “/home/user/network_logs/ping.log” exists and `stat --printf="%s"` is greater than zero.
    3. “/home/user/network_logs/last_run.txt” exists, `wc -l` outputs exactly “5”.
       3.1. `head -n 1 /home/user/network_logs/last_run.txt` must be the literal string:  
            Last 4 lines of ping.log:
    4. `crontab -l` produces a line that matches the regexp  
         ^\*/2 \* \* \* \* ping -c 1 127\.0\.0\.1 >> /home/user/network_logs/ping\.log 2>&1$
name: task_000000_f2f58b59
